request.question: What was the record for the head football coach of this private four-year Christian-based college founded in Salina, Kansas, United States?

title: Tim McCarty
Kansas Wesleyan University
Saint Joseph Seminary College
Willie Fritz
Ron Dupree
C. L. Williams
Coach Miller
Mark Raymond
Carl Anderson (American football)
Tom Keele
sentences: Tim McCarty is an American football coach and former player.
He is currently the head football coach at East Central University in Ada, Oklahoma, a position he first held from 2004 to 2005 and then resume in 2009.
From 1999 to 2003 McCarty was the head football coach at Tabor College in Hillsboro, Kansas.
His two tenures at East Central were sepeated by a three-year stint at the assistant head football coach at Kansas State University.
Kansas Wesleyan University is a private four-year Christian-based college founded in Salina, Kansas, United States in 1886.
It is affiliated with the United Methodist Church.
About 850 students attend the 28 acre campus.

The university is accredited by the North Central Association of Colleges and Schools, the National League for Nursing, the National Council for Accreditation of Teacher Education (NCATE), and the Kansas State Department of Education.
Saint Joseph Seminary College, also known as St. Ben or St. Ben's, is a private four-year seminary college founded in 1891.
It is operated by the Benedictine Monks of Saint Joseph Abbey and the dioceses of the ecclesiastical provinces of New Orleans and Mobile, Alabama.
The 1200 acre piney woods campus is located in St. Benedict, Louisiana, north of Covington.
Willie Fritz (born April 2, 1960) is an American football coach and former player.
He is the current head coach at Tulane University.
From 2014 to 2015, he was head coach at Georgia Southern University.
From 2010 to 2013, he was the head football coach at Sam Houston State University.
From 1997 to 2009, Fritz served as the head football coach at the University of Central Missouri.
From 1993 to 1996, he was the head football coach at Blinn College, a junior college in Brenham, Texas.
Ron Dupree is a former American football coach.
Dupree was the 15th head football coach at Kansas Wesleyan University in Salina, Kansas, serving for three seasons, from 1979 to 1980 and again in 1996.
His coaching record at Kansas Wesleyan was 11–18.
C. L. Williams was an American football coach.
He was the third head football coach at Kansas Wesleyan University in Salina, Kansas, serving for two seasons, from 1908 to 1909, compiling a record of 9–5.
Coach Miller was an American football coach.
He was the second head football coach at Kansas Wesleyan University in Salina, Kansas, serving for one season, in 1906, and compiling a record of 0–1.
Mark Raymond is an American football coach, currently serving as the head football coach at Williams College.
Raymond served as the head football coach at St. Lawrence University from 2010 to 2015, winning two conference titles and compiling an overall record of 31–30.
Raymond was named Liberty League Coach of the Year following the conclusion of both the 2010 and 2015 seasons at St. Lawrence.
Raymond was named head football coach at Williams College on February 24, 2016.
Carl Rudolph Frederick "Swede" Anderson IV (September 9, 1898 – April 30, 1978) was an American college football coach at Western Kentucky University and Howard Payne University.
Anderson graduated from Centre College in Danville, Kentucky in 1924, where he played in the backfield with legendary alumnus Bo McMillin.
Anderson then followed McMillin to Centenary College of Louisiana and Geneva College.
Anderson then served one year as the head football coach at Western Kentucky, before moving to Kansas State as its freshman team coach in 1930.
Anderson returned to Western Kentucky as its head coach from 1934 to 1937.
He was the backfield coach under McMillin at Indiana from 1938 to 1945.
He then returned to his alma mater, Centre College, where he coached the Praying Colonels until 1950.
The following season, Anderson became the seventh head football coach at the Howard Payne University in Brownwood, Texas and held that position from 1951 to 1952.
His coaching record at Howard Payne was 7–10.
Tom Keele (born c. 1933) is a former American football coach.
He served as the head football coach at California State University, Northridge from 1979 to 1985, compiling a record of 31–42–1.
Keele graduated from Jefferson High School in Portland Oregon in 1951.
He attended the University of Oregon, where he played football for the Oregon Webfoots as a tackle from 1957 to 1959.
Keele began his coaching career in 1960 at North Eugene High School in Eugene, Oregon, working two years as an assistant football coach and sophomore basketball coach.
He moved to Oregon City High School in Oregon City, Oregon in 1962, serving as head football coach and leading his team to a 9–1–1 record.
The following year, he was hired as head football coach at the newly-formed Sheldon High School in Eugene.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: What was the record for the head football coach of this private four-year Christian-based college founded in Salina, Kansas, United States?
hop1_docs: Kansas Wesleyan University | Kansas Wesleyan University is a private four-year Christian-based college founded in Salina, Kansas, United States in 1886.  It is affiliated with the United Methodist Church.  About 850 students attend the 28 acre campus.  The university is accredited by the North Central Association of Colleges and Schools, the National League for Nursing, the National Council for Accreditation of Teacher Education (NCATE), and the Kansas State Department of Education.
Bethel College (Kansas) | Bethel College is a four-year private Christian liberal arts college in North Newton, Kansas, United States.  It is affiliated with the Mennonite Church USA.

Coach Miller | Coach Miller was an American football coach.  He was the second head football coach at Kansas Wesleyan University in Salina, Kansas, serving for one season, in 1906, and compiling a record of 0–1.

1908 Kansas State Aggies football team | The 1908 Kansas State Aggies football team represented Kansas State Agricultural College (now Kansas State University) in the 1908 college football season.  In their fourth year under head coach Mike Ahearn, the Aggies compiled a 6–2 record, and outscored their opponents by a combined total of 182 to 74.

1909 Kansas State Aggies football team | The 1909 Kansas State Aggies football team represented Kansas State Agricultural College (now Kansas State University) in the 1909 college football season.  In their fifth year under head coach Mike Ahearn, the Aggies compiled a 7–2 record, and outscored their opponents by a combined total of 320 to 11.

Matt Drinkall | Matt Drinkall is an American football coach.  In January 2014 he was hired as the head coach of the Kansas Wesleyan Coyotes in Salina, Kansas.  He has worked previously as a football coach at the high school and college level.

W. F. Ragle | W. F. Ragle was an American football coach.  He was the fifth head football coach at Kansas Wesleyan University in Salina, Kansas, serving for one season, in 1915 and compiling a record of 5–4.

Header: Kansas Wesleyan University — head football coach records (cited passages)

- Coach candidates and concrete records in the passages:
  - "Coach Miller" — "second head football coach" at Kansas Wesleyan; "serving for one season, in 1906," "compiling a record of 0–1."
  - "W. F. Ragle" — "fifth head football coach" at Kansas Wesleyan; "serving for one season, in 1915" and "compiling a record of 5–4."
  - "Matt Drinkall" — "hired as the head coach of the Kansas Wesleyan Coyotes in Salina, Kansas" in "January 2014" (no record given).
- Ambiguity / missing facts:
  - Ambiguous target: the phrase "the head football coach" could refer to multiple persons (Coach Miller, W. F. Ragle, Matt Drinkall). Label: multiple candidate coaches.
  - Missing fact: the win–loss record for the current head coach (Matt Drinkall) is not provided in the passages. Bridge entity: "Matt Drinkall" / "Kansas Wesleyan Coyotes football".
- Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
  1. Kansas Wesleyan Coyotes football — inspect lede/infobox and "Head coaches" or "Coaching history" sections (to get official head coach list and records).
  2. Matt Drinkall — fetch full page, check "Career" or "Head coaching record" section for his win–loss record.
  3. "Coach Miller (Kansas Wesleyan)" and "W. F. Ragle" pages — verify historical records and context if a historical coach is intended.

Kansas Wesleyan Coyotes | The Kansas Wesleyan Coyotes are the athletic teams that represent Kansas Wesleyan University in Salina, Kansas.  They are part of the National Association of Intercollegiate Athletics (NAIA), competing in the Kansas Collegiate Athletic Conference (KCAC).

Gene Bissell Field | Gene Bissell Field is the Salina, Kansas home field for the Kansas Wesleyan University Coyotes football and soccer teams.  The field was named in honor of former head coach Franklin Gene Bissell, who attended the opening day celebration on October 3, 2015.
